[[co:commons:Category:Takamatsu Castle (Bitchu)]][[en:Takamatsu Castle (Bitchū)]][[es:Castillo Takamatsu (Bitchu)]][[fa:قلعه تاکاماتسو]][[fr:Château de Takamatsu (Bitchu)]][[ja:高松城 (備中国)]][[ko:다카마쓰성 (오카야마현)]][[pt:Castelo de Takamatsu (Bitchu)]][[zh:高松城 (備中國)]] [[co:commons:Category:Takamatsu Castle (Bitchu)]][[en:Takamatsu Castle (Bitchū)]][[es:Castillo Takamatsu (Bitchu)]][[fa:قلعه تاکاماتسو]][[fr:Château de Takamatsu (Bitchu)]][[ja:高松城 (備中国)]][[ko:다카마쓰성 (오카야마현)]][[pt:Castelo de Takamatsu (Bitchu)]][[zh:高松城 (備中國)]] [[sn:commons:Category:Takamatsu Castle (Bitchu)]][[en:Takamatsu Castle (Bitchū)]][[es:Castillo Takamatsu (Bitchu)]][[fa:قلعه تاکاماتسو]][[fr:Château de Takamatsu (Bitchu)]][[ja:高松城 (備中国)]][[ko:다카마쓰성 (오카야마현)]][[pt:Castelo de Takamatsu (Bitchu)]][[zh:高松城 (備中國)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1071073]] Wikidata name: Takamatsu Castle [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:44, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{for|the Takamatsu Castle in Sanuki|Takamatsu Castle (Sanuki)}} {{Infobox military installation | name=Takamatsu Castle | nativename-a= 高松城 | partof= | location={{ill|Okayama|en|Okayama|WD=Q200078|lt=Okayama}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | image=Bitchu Takamatsu castle ruins.jpg | image_size=250px | caption=Honmaru Base and Moat of Takamatsu Castle | type={{ill|Japanese castle|en|Japanese castle|WD=Q92026|lt=Azuchi-Momoyama castle}} | map_type = Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | map_relief = 1 | map_caption = Takamatsu Castle | map_size = 270px |coordinates = {{coord|34|41|34.8|N|133|49|19.36|E|region:JP_scale:10000_source:jawiki|display=inline}} | height= | ownership= | controlledby={{ill|Ishikawa clan|en|Ishikawa clan|WD=Q615039|lt=Ishikawa clan}} (until 1575), {{ill|Mōri clan|en|Mōri clan|WD=Q914637|lt=Mōri clan}} (1575–1582), {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} (1582–1598), [[Hanabusa clan]] (c. 1598 – mid-17th century) | condition=ruins | built=late 16th century | builder=[[Mimura clan]] | used=late 16th to mid-17th centuries | materials=Wood, stone, plaster | demolished= | battles={{ill|Siege of Takamatsu|en|Siege of Takamatsu|WD=Q1046201|lt=Siege of Takamatsu}} (1582) | events= | past_commanders={{ill|Shimizu Muneharu|en|Shimizu Muneharu|WD=Q1046032|lt=Shimizu Muneharu}} (c. 1575–1582)
[[Hanabusa Masanari]] | garrison= | occupants= |footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} in his 1582 {{ill|Siege of Takamatsu|en|Siege of Takamatsu|WD=Q1046201|lt=siege}} of the castle]] {{nihongo|'''Takamatsu Castle'''|高松城|Takamatsu-jō}} of {{ill|Bitchū Province|en|Bitchū Province|WD=Q1047514|lt=Bitchū Province}} was a {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}} {{ill|Japanese castle|en|Japanese castle|WD=Q92026|lt=Japanese castle}} located in what is today the Kayo neighborhood of {{ill|Kita-ku, Okayama|en|Kita-ku, Okayama|WD=Q855478|lt=Kita-ku, Okayama}} in {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture}}.{{cite web|URL=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%82%99%E4%B8%AD%E9%AB%98%E6%9D%BE%E5%9F%8E-180261|title= 備中高松城（読み）びっちゅうたかまつじょう百科事典マイペディア「備中高松城」の解説|publisher=kotobank|access-date=15 October 2021}} The style of the castle was a ''hirajō'' (flatland castle) with no stone walls, but only earthen walls. The castle was surrounded by marshes, which formed a natural moat. Its ruins have been protected by the central government as a [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 1902.{{cite web|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2260|title=高松城跡 附 水攻築提跡 |work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 December 2016}} ==History== It is not clear when a castle was built at this location, which is located between central Okayama city and the Takahashi River. Historically this area had been a granary area and the main base of ancient [[Kingdom of Kibi]]. The route of the {{ill|San'yōdō|en|San'yōdō|WD=Q1376722|lt=San'yōdō}} highway which connected {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} with {{ill|Shimonoseki|en|Shimonoseki|WD=Q203308|lt=Shimonoseki}} passed through natural levee just at south of the castle. During the early Sengoku period, the Mimura clan ruled the entire Bitchu region from Bitchu-Matsuyama Castle, and the Mimura ordered their vassals, the Ishikawa clan, to build a fortification at this site. The Ishikawa and Mimura were destroyed by the {{ill|Mōri clan|en|Mōri clan|WD=Q914637|lt=Mōri clan}} in 1575, and the castle was awarded to {{ill|Shimizu Muneharu|en|Shimizu Muneharu|WD=Q1046032|lt=Shimizu Muneharu}}, a senior vassal of the Ishikawa who had defected to the Mōri. In 1582, {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Hashiba Hideyoshi}} was ordered to conquer western Japan by {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}} and laid {{ill|Siege of Takamatsu|en|Siege of Takamatsu|WD=Q1046201|lt=siege to Takamatsu Castle}}. As Takamatsu Castle was surrounded by marshes, it was considered impregnable to a normal assault and was expected to be able to withstand a prolonged siege. However, Hideyoshi adopted an innovative approach at the suggestion of his strategist [[:en:Kuroda Kanbei|Kuroda Kanbei]]. By paying extravagant amounts of money for round-the-clock efforts, he completed a three-kilometer long dike, damming the outlets to the marshes within 11 days. Aided by the arrival of the {{ill|East Asian rainy season|en|East Asian rainy season|WD=Q658486|lt=rainy season}}, the castle was soon flooded. Shimizu Muneharu was forced to surrender, committing {{ill|Seppuku|en|Seppuku|WD=Q133462|lt=Seppuku}} in exchange for the lives of his troops. Following this siege, and the rise of Toyotomi Hideyoshi to power after the assassination of Oda Nobunaga, the castle became part of the territory of {{ill|Ukita Hideie|en|Ukita Hideie|WD=Q1329792|lt=Ukita Hideie}}, who assigned it to his ''{{ill|Karō|en|Karō|WD=Q1316445|lt=Karō}}'', [[Hanabusa Masashige]]. Following the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara}} in 1600, the Ukita clan sided with the losing Western army; however, the [[Hanabusa clan]] fought alongside the Eastern Army of {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, and were awarded ''{{ill|Hatamoto|en|Hatamoto|WD=Q572662|lt=Hatamoto}}'' status as direct retainers of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. Some years later the Hanabusa relocated from Takamatsu to Abe, in what is today {{ill|Sōja|en|Sōja|WD=Q873536|lt=Sōja}} city and the castle was abandoned. Today, only a portion of the dam built by Hideyoshi and a small part of the castle's masonry has survived, and almost no trace of the castle remains. The ruins include the alleged Kofuns for Shimizu Muneharu's head, and for his body. The site has been maintained as the {{nihongo|'''Takamatsu Castle Siege Historic Site Park'''|高松城水攻め史跡公園|Takamatsu-jō mizuzeme shiseki kōen}}, with markers, such as wooden posts, show the area where the castle, dikes, and siege equipment were once located and where Shimizu Muneharu committed suicide. On April 6, 2017 Bitchu Takamatsu Castle was designated by the Japanese Castle Foundation as Okayama Prefecture's only entry on the [[:en:Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles|Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles]] (続日本100名城). The list adds to the original Top 100 list totaling 200 castles. Bitchu Takamatsu Castle is listed as #171.{{cite web|url= http://jokaku.jp/japan-top-100-castles/best-100-castles-of-japan-2nd-selection/ |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 July 2019}} The castle ruins are a ten minutes walk from {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Kibi Line|en|Kibi Line|WD=Q844682|lt=Kibi Line}} [[:en:Bitchu-Takamatsu Station|Bitchu-Takamatsu Station]]. == In popular culture == Takamatsu Castle was featured in the video game ''{{ill|Soulcalibur (video game)|en|Soulcalibur (video game)|WD=Q1060715|lt=Soulcalibur}}'' by [[Namco]], used as the fighting stage for the character {{ill|Heishiro Mitsurugi|en|Heishiro Mitsurugi|WD=Q60533066|lt=Heishiro Mitsurugi}}. The game contains two versions of the stage, one set during the siege and the other set in the winter following it. ==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Okayama)]] == Literature == *{{cite book |title=An Encyclopedia of Japanese Castles | last=De Lange |first=William |year=2021 |pages=600 pages|publisher=Toyo Press |location=Groningen |isbn=978-9492722300}} * Sansom, George (1961). ''A History of Japan: 1334–1615''. Stanford, California: Stanford University Press * Turnbull, Stephen (1998). ''The Samurai Sourcebook''. London: Cassell & Co. == References == {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category-inline|Takamatsu Castle (Bitchu)}} *[https://www.pref.okayama.jp/site/kodai/628728.html Okayama Ancient Kibi Cultural Properties Center] {{Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles}} [[file:Kawazugahana Embankment base.jpg|thumb|An excavated portion of the dike and embankment base constructed by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} [[en:Takamatsu Castle (Bitchū)]] {{Commons category|Takamatsu Castle (Bitchu)}} [[es:Castillo Takamatsu (Bitchu)]] [[fa:قلعه تاکاماتسو]] [[fr:Château de Takamatsu (Bitchu)]] [[ja:高松城 (備中国)]] [[ko:다카마쓰성 (오카야마현)]] [[pt:Castelo de Takamatsu (Bitchu)]] [[zh:高松城 (備中國)]] {{wikidata link|Q1071073}} [[Category:Bitchū Province]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Okayama]] [[Category:Castles in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Mōri clan]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Ruined castles in Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

The game contains two versions of the stage, one set during the siege and the other set in the winter following it. ==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Okayama)]] == Literature == *{{cite book |title=An Encyclopedia of Japanese Castles | last=De Lange |first=William |year=2021 |pages=600 pages|publisher=Toyo Press |location=Groningen |isbn=978-9492722300}} * Sansom, George (1961). ''A History of Japan: 1334–1615''. Stanford, California: Stanford University Press * Turnbull, Stephen (1998). ''The Samurai Sourcebook''. London: Cassell & Co. == References == {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category-inline|Takamatsu Castle (Bitchu)}} *[https://www.pref.okayama.jp/site/kodai/628728.html Okayama Ancient Kibi Cultural Properties Center] {{Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles}} [[file:Kawazugahana Embankment base.jpg|thumb|An excavated portion of the dike and embankment base constructed by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} [[en:Takamatsu Castle (Bitchū)]] {{Commons category|Takamatsu Castle (Bitchu)}} [[es:Castillo Takamatsu (Bitchu)]] [[fa:قلعه تاکاماتسو]] [[fr:Château de Takamatsu (Bitchu)]] [[ja:高松城 (備中国)]] [[ko:다카마쓰성 (오카야마현)]] [[pt:Castelo de Takamatsu (Bitchu)]] [[zh:高松城 (備中國)]] {{wikidata link|Q1071073}} [[Category:Bitchū Province]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Okayama]] [[Category:Castles in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Mōri clan]] [[Category:Ruined castles in Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

[[co:commons:Category:Takamatsu Castle (Bitchu)]][[en:Takamatsu Castle (Bitchū)]][[es:Castillo Takamatsu (Bitchu)]][[fa:قلعه تاکاماتسو]][[fr:Château de Takamatsu (Bitchu)]][[ja:高松城 (備中国)]][[ko:다카마쓰성 (오카야마현)]][[pt:Castelo de Takamatsu (Bitchu)]][[zh:高松城 (備中國)]] [[co:commons:Category:Takamatsu Castle (Bitchu)]][[en:Takamatsu Castle (Bitchū)]][[es:Castillo Takamatsu (Bitchu)]][[fa:قلعه تاکاماتسو]][[fr:Château de Takamatsu (Bitchu)]][[ja:高松城 (備中国)]][[ko:다카마쓰성 (오카야마현)]][[pt:Castelo de Takamatsu (Bitchu)]][[zh:高松城 (備中國)]] [[sn:commons:Category:Takamatsu Castle (Bitchu)]][[en:Takamatsu Castle (Bitchū)]][[es:Castillo Takamatsu (Bitchu)]][[fa:قلعه تاکاماتسو]][[fr:Château de Takamatsu (Bitchu)]][[ja:高松城 (備中国)]][[ko:다카마쓰성 (오카야마현)]][[pt:Castelo de Takamatsu (Bitchu)]][[zh:高松城 (備中國)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1071073]] Wikidata name: Takamatsu Castle [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:44, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{for|the Takamatsu Castle in Sanuki|Takamatsu Castle (Sanuki)}} {{Infobox military installation | name=Takamatsu Castle | nativename-a= 高松城 | partof= | location={{ill|Okayama|en|Okayama|WD=Q200078|lt=Okayama}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | image=Bitchu Takamatsu castle ruins.jpg | image_size=250px | caption=Honmaru Base and Moat of Takamatsu Castle | type={{ill|Japanese castle|en|Japanese castle|WD=Q92026|lt=Azuchi-Momoyama castle}} | map_type = Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | map_relief = 1 | map_caption = Takamatsu Castle | map_size = 270px |coordinates = {{coord|34|41|34.8|N|133|49|19.36|E|region:JP_scale:10000_source:jawiki|display=inline}} | height= | ownership= | controlledby={{ill|Ishikawa clan|en|Ishikawa clan|WD=Q615039|lt=Ishikawa clan}} (until 1575), {{ill|Mōri clan|en|Mōri clan|WD=Q914637|lt=Mōri clan}} (1575–1582), {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} (1582–1598), [[Hanabusa clan]] (c. 1598 – mid-17th century) | condition=ruins | built=late 16th century | builder=[[Mimura clan]] | used=late 16th to mid-17th centuries | materials=Wood, stone, plaster | demolished= | battles={{ill|Siege of Takamatsu|en|Siege of Takamatsu|WD=Q1046201|lt=Siege of Takamatsu}} (1582) | events= | past_commanders={{ill|Shimizu Muneharu|en|Shimizu Muneharu|WD=Q1046032|lt=Shimizu Muneharu}} (c. 1575–1582)
[[Hanabusa Masanari]] | garrison= | occupants= |footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} in his 1582 {{ill|Siege of Takamatsu|en|Siege of Takamatsu|WD=Q1046201|lt=siege}} of the castle]] {{nihongo|'''Takamatsu Castle'''|高松城|Takamatsu-jō}} of {{ill|Bitchū Province|en|Bitchū Province|WD=Q1047514|lt=Bitchū Province}} was a {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}} {{ill|Japanese castle|en|Japanese castle|WD=Q92026|lt=Japanese castle}} located in what is today the Kayo neighborhood of {{ill|Kita-ku, Okayama|en|Kita-ku, Okayama|WD=Q855478|lt=Kita-ku, Okayama}} in {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture}}.{{cite web|URL=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%82%99%E4%B8%AD%E9%AB%98%E6%9D%BE%E5%9F%8E-180261|title= 備中高松城（読み）びっちゅうたかまつじょう百科事典マイペディア「備中高松城」の解説|publisher=kotobank|access-date=15 October 2021}} The style of the castle was a ''hirajō'' (flatland castle) with no stone walls, but only earthen walls. The castle was surrounded by marshes, which formed a natural moat. Its ruins have been protected by the central government as a [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 1902.{{cite web|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2260|title=高松城跡 附 水攻築提跡 |work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 December 2016}} ==History== It is not clear when a castle was built at this location, which is located between central Okayama city and the Takahashi River. Historically this area had been a granary area and the main base of ancient [[Kingdom of Kibi]]. The route of the {{ill|San'yōdō|en|San'yōdō|WD=Q1376722|lt=San'yōdō}} highway which connected {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} with {{ill|Shimonoseki|en|Shimonoseki|WD=Q203308|lt=Shimonoseki}} passed through natural levee just at south of the castle. During the early Sengoku period, the Mimura clan ruled the entire Bitchu region from Bitchu-Matsuyama Castle, and the Mimura ordered their vassals, the Ishikawa clan, to build a fortification at this site. The Ishikawa and Mimura were destroyed by the {{ill|Mōri clan|en|Mōri clan|WD=Q914637|lt=Mōri clan}} in 1575, and the castle was awarded to {{ill|Shimizu Muneharu|en|Shimizu Muneharu|WD=Q1046032|lt=Shimizu Muneharu}}, a senior vassal of the Ishikawa who had defected to the Mōri. In 1582, {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Hashiba Hideyoshi}} was ordered to conquer western Japan by {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}} and laid {{ill|Siege of Takamatsu|en|Siege of Takamatsu|WD=Q1046201|lt=siege to Takamatsu Castle}}. As Takamatsu Castle was surrounded by marshes, it was considered impregnable to a normal assault and was expected to be able to withstand a prolonged siege. However, Hideyoshi adopted an innovative approach at the suggestion of his strategist [[:en:Kuroda Kanbei|Kuroda Kanbei]]. By paying extravagant amounts of money for round-the-clock efforts, he completed a three-kilometer long dike, damming the outlets to the marshes within 11 days. Aided by the arrival of the {{ill|East Asian rainy season|en|East Asian rainy season|WD=Q658486|lt=rainy season}}, the castle was soon flooded. Shimizu Muneharu was forced to surrender, committing {{ill|Seppuku|en|Seppuku|WD=Q133462|lt=Seppuku}} in exchange for the lives of his troops. Following this siege, and the rise of Toyotomi Hideyoshi to power after the assassination of Oda Nobunaga, the castle became part of the territory of {{ill|Ukita Hideie|en|Ukita Hideie|WD=Q1329792|lt=Ukita Hideie}}, who assigned it to his ''{{ill|Karō|en|Karō|WD=Q1316445|lt=Karō}}'', [[Hanabusa Masashige]]. Following the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara}} in 1600, the Ukita clan sided with the losing Western army; however, the [[Hanabusa clan]] fought alongside the Eastern Army of {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, and were awarded ''{{ill|Hatamoto|en|Hatamoto|WD=Q572662|lt=Hatamoto}}'' status as direct retainers of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. Some years later the Hanabusa relocated from Takamatsu to Abe, in what is today {{ill|Sōja|en|Sōja|WD=Q873536|lt=Sōja}} city and the castle was abandoned. Today, only a portion of the dam built by Hideyoshi and a small part of the castle's masonry has survived, and almost no trace of the castle remains. The ruins include the alleged Kofuns for Shimizu Muneharu's head, and for his body. The site has been maintained as the {{nihongo|'''Takamatsu Castle Siege Historic Site Park'''|高松城水攻め史跡公園|Takamatsu-jō mizuzeme shiseki kōen}}, with markers, such as wooden posts, show the area where the castle, dikes, and siege equipment were once located and where Shimizu Muneharu committed suicide. On April 6, 2017 Bitchu Takamatsu Castle was designated by the Japanese Castle Foundation as Okayama Prefecture's only entry on the [[:en:Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles|Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles]] (続日本100名城). The list adds to the original Top 100 list totaling 200 castles. Bitchu Takamatsu Castle is listed as #171.{{cite web|url= http://jokaku.jp/japan-top-100-castles/best-100-castles-of-japan-2nd-selection/ |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 July 2019}} The castle ruins are a ten minutes walk from {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Kibi Line|en|Kibi Line|WD=Q844682|lt=Kibi Line}} [[:en:Bitchu-Takamatsu Station|Bitchu-Takamatsu Station]]. == In popular culture == Takamatsu Castle was featured in the video game ''{{ill|Soulcalibur (video game)|en|Soulcalibur (video game)|WD=Q1060715|lt=Soulcalibur}}'' by [[Namco]], used as the fighting stage for the character {{ill|Heishiro Mitsurugi|en|Heishiro Mitsurugi|WD=Q60533066|lt=Heishiro Mitsurugi}}. The game contains two versions of the stage, one set during the siege and the other set in the winter following it. ==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Okayama)]] == Literature == *{{cite book |title=An Encyclopedia of Japanese Castles | last=De Lange |first=William |year=2021 |pages=600 pages|publisher=Toyo Press |location=Groningen |isbn=978-9492722300}} * Sansom, George (1961). ''A History of Japan: 1334–1615''. Stanford, California: Stanford University Press * Turnbull, Stephen (1998). ''The Samurai Sourcebook''. London: Cassell & Co. == References == {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category-inline|Takamatsu Castle (Bitchu)}} *[https://www.pref.okayama.jp/site/kodai/628728.html Okayama Ancient Kibi Cultural Properties Center] {{Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles}} [[file:Kawazugahana Embankment base.jpg|thumb|An excavated portion of the dike and embankment base constructed by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} [[en:Takamatsu Castle (Bitchū)]] {{Commons category|Takamatsu Castle (Bitchu)}} [[es:Castillo Takamatsu (Bitchu)]] [[fa:قلعه تاکاماتسو]] [[fr:Château de Takamatsu (Bitchu)]] [[ja:高松城 (備中国)]] [[ko:다카마쓰성 (오카야마현)]] [[pt:Castelo de Takamatsu (Bitchu)]] [[zh:高松城 (備中國)]] {{wikidata link|Q1071073}} [[Category:Bitchū Province]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Okayama]] [[Category:Castles in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Mōri clan]] [[Category:Ruined castles in Japan]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

(오카야마현)]][[pt:Castelo de Takamatsu (Bitchu)]][[zh:高松城 (備中國)]] [[co:commons:Category:Takamatsu Castle (Bitchu)]][[en:Takamatsu Castle (Bitchū)]][[es:Castillo Takamatsu (Bitchu)]][[fa:قلعه تاکاماتسو]][[fr:Château de Takamatsu (Bitchu)]][[ja:高松城 (備中国)]][[ko:다카마쓰성 (오카야마현)]][[pt:Castelo de Takamatsu (Bitchu)]][[zh:高松城 (備中國)]] [[sn:commons:Category:Takamatsu Castle (Bitchu)]][[en:Takamatsu Castle (Bitchū)]][[es:Castillo Takamatsu (Bitchu)]][[fa:قلعه تاکاماتسو]][[fr:Château de Takamatsu (Bitchu)]][[ja:高松城 (備中国)]][[ko:다카마쓰성 (오카야마현)]][[pt:Castelo de Takamatsu (Bitchu)]][[zh:高松城 (備中國)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1071073]] Wikidata name: Takamatsu Castle [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:44, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{for|the Takamatsu Castle in Sanuki|Takamatsu Castle (Sanuki)}} {{Infobox military installation | name=Takamatsu Castle | nativename-a= 高松城 | partof= | location={{ill|Okayama|en|Okayama|WD=Q200078|lt=Okayama}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | image=Bitchu Takamatsu castle ruins.jpg | image_size=250px | caption=Honmaru Base and Moat of Takamatsu Castle | type={{ill|Japanese castle|en|Japanese castle|WD=Q92026|lt=Azuchi-Momoyama castle}} | map_type = Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | map_relief = 1 | map_caption = Takamatsu Castle | map_size = 270px |coordinates = {{coord|34|41|34.8|N|133|49|19.36|E|region:JP_scale:10000_source:jawiki|display=inline}} | height= | ownership= | controlledby={{ill|Ishikawa clan|en|Ishikawa clan|WD=Q615039|lt=Ishikawa clan}} (until 1575), {{ill|Mōri clan|en|Mōri clan|WD=Q914637|lt=Mōri clan}} (1575–1582), {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} (1582–1598), [[Hanabusa clan]] (c. 1598 – mid-17th century) | condition=ruins | built=late 16th century | builder=[[Mimura clan]] | used=late 16th to mid-17th centuries | materials=Wood, stone, plaster | demolished= | battles={{ill|Siege of Takamatsu|en|Siege of Takamatsu|WD=Q1046201|lt=Siege of Takamatsu}} (1582) | events= | past_commanders={{ill|Shimizu Muneharu|en|Shimizu Muneharu|WD=Q1046032|lt=Shimizu Muneharu}} (c. 1575–1582)
[[Hanabusa Masanari]] | garrison= | occupants= |footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} in his 1582 {{ill|Siege of Takamatsu|en|Siege of Takamatsu|WD=Q1046201|lt=siege}} of the castle]] {{nihongo|'''Takamatsu Castle'''|高松城|Takamatsu-jō}} of {{ill|Bitchū Province|en|Bitchū Province|WD=Q1047514|lt=Bitchū Province}} was a {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}} {{ill|Japanese castle|en|Japanese castle|WD=Q92026|lt=Japanese castle}} located in what is today the Kayo neighborhood of {{ill|Kita-ku, Okayama|en|Kita-ku, Okayama|WD=Q855478|lt=Kita-ku, Okayama}} in {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture}}.{{cite web|URL=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%82%99%E4%B8%AD%E9%AB%98%E6%9D%BE%E5%9F%8E-180261|title= 備中高松城（読み）びっちゅうたかまつじょう百科事典マイペディア「備中高松城」の解説|publisher=kotobank|access-date=15 October 2021}} The style of the castle was a ''hirajō'' (flatland castle) with no stone walls, but only earthen walls. The castle was surrounded by marshes, which formed a natural moat. Its ruins have been protected by the central government as a [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 1902.{{cite web|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2260|title=高松城跡 附 水攻築提跡 |work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 December 2016}} ==History== It is not clear when a castle was built at this location, which is located between central Okayama city and the Takahashi River. Historically this area had been a granary area and the main base of ancient [[Kingdom of Kibi]]. The route of the {{ill|San'yōdō|en|San'yōdō|WD=Q1376722|lt=San'yōdō}} highway which connected {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} with {{ill|Shimonoseki|en|Shimonoseki|WD=Q203308|lt=Shimonoseki}} passed through natural levee just at south of the castle. During the early Sengoku period, the Mimura clan ruled the entire Bitchu region from Bitchu-Matsuyama Castle, and the Mimura ordered their vassals, the Ishikawa clan, to build a fortification at this site. The Ishikawa and Mimura were destroyed by the {{ill|Mōri clan|en|Mōri clan|WD=Q914637|lt=Mōri clan}} in 1575, and the castle was awarded to {{ill|Shimizu Muneharu|en|Shimizu Muneharu|WD=Q1046032|lt=Shimizu Muneharu}}, a senior vassal of the Ishikawa who had defected to the Mōri. In 1582, {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Hashiba Hideyoshi}} was ordered to conquer western Japan by {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}} and laid {{ill|Siege of Takamatsu|en|Siege of Takamatsu|WD=Q1046201|lt=siege to Takamatsu Castle}}. As Takamatsu Castle was surrounded by marshes, it was considered impregnable to a normal assault and was expected to be able to withstand a prolonged siege. However, Hideyoshi adopted an innovative approach at the suggestion of his strategist [[:en:Kuroda Kanbei|Kuroda Kanbei]]. By paying extravagant amounts of money for round-the-clock efforts, he completed a three-kilometer long dike, damming the outlets to the marshes within 11 days. Aided by the arrival of the {{ill|East Asian rainy season|en|East Asian rainy season|WD=Q658486|lt=rainy season}}, the castle was soon flooded. Shimizu Muneharu was forced to surrender, committing {{ill|Seppuku|en|Seppuku|WD=Q133462|lt=Seppuku}} in exchange for the lives of his troops. Following this siege, and the rise of Toyotomi Hideyoshi to power after the assassination of Oda Nobunaga, the castle became part of the territory of {{ill|Ukita Hideie|en|Ukita Hideie|WD=Q1329792|lt=Ukita Hideie}}, who assigned it to his ''{{ill|Karō|en|Karō|WD=Q1316445|lt=Karō}}'', [[Hanabusa Masashige]]. Following the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara}} in 1600, the Ukita clan sided with the losing Western army; however, the [[Hanabusa clan]] fought alongside the Eastern Army of {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, and were awarded ''{{ill|Hatamoto|en|Hatamoto|WD=Q572662|lt=Hatamoto}}'' status as direct retainers of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. Some years later the Hanabusa relocated from Takamatsu to Abe, in what is today {{ill|Sōja|en|Sōja|WD=Q873536|lt=Sōja}} city and the castle was abandoned. Today, only a portion of the dam built by Hideyoshi and a small part of the castle's masonry has survived, and almost no trace of the castle remains. The ruins include the alleged Kofuns for Shimizu Muneharu's head, and for his body. The site has been maintained as the {{nihongo|'''Takamatsu Castle Siege Historic Site Park'''|高松城水攻め史跡公園|Takamatsu-jō mizuzeme shiseki kōen}}, with markers, such as wooden posts, show the area where the castle, dikes, and siege equipment were once located and where Shimizu Muneharu committed suicide. On April 6, 2017 Bitchu Takamatsu Castle was designated by the Japanese Castle Foundation as Okayama Prefecture's only entry on the [[:en:Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles|Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles]] (続日本100名城). The list adds to the original Top 100 list totaling 200 castles. Bitchu Takamatsu Castle is listed as #171.{{cite web|url= http://jokaku.jp/japan-top-100-castles/best-100-castles-of-japan-2nd-selection/ |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 July 2019}} The castle ruins are a ten minutes walk from {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Kibi Line|en|Kibi Line|WD=Q844682|lt=Kibi Line}} [[:en:Bitchu-Takamatsu Station|Bitchu-Takamatsu Station]]. == In popular culture == Takamatsu Castle was featured in the video game ''{{ill|Soulcalibur (video game)|en|Soulcalibur (video game)|WD=Q1060715|lt=Soulcalibur}}'' by [[Namco]], used as the fighting stage for the character {{ill|Heishiro Mitsurugi|en|Heishiro Mitsurugi|WD=Q60533066|lt=Heishiro Mitsurugi}}. The game contains two versions of the stage, one set during the siege and the other set in the winter following it. ==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Okayama)]] == Literature == *{{cite book |title=An Encyclopedia of Japanese Castles | last=De Lange |first=William |year=2021 |pages=600 pages|publisher=Toyo Press |location=Groningen |isbn=978-9492722300}} * Sansom, George (1961). ''A History of Japan: 1334–1615''. Stanford, California: Stanford University Press * Turnbull, Stephen (1998). ''The Samurai Sourcebook''. London: Cassell & Co. == References == {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category-inline|Takamatsu Castle (Bitchu)}} *[https://www.pref.okayama.jp/site/kodai/628728.html Okayama Ancient Kibi Cultural Properties Center] {{Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles}} [[file:Kawazugahana Embankment base.jpg|thumb|An excavated portion of the dike and embankment base constructed by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} [[en:Takamatsu Castle (Bitchū)]] {{Commons category|Takamatsu Castle (Bitchu)}} [[es:Castillo Takamatsu (Bitchu)]] [[fa:قلعه تاکاماتسو]] [[fr:Château de Takamatsu (Bitchu)]] [[ja:高松城 (備中国)]] [[ko:다카마쓰성 (오카야마현)]] [[pt:Castelo de Takamatsu (Bitchu)]] [[zh:高松城 (備中國)]] {{wikidata link|Q1071073}} [[Category:Bitchū Province]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Okayama]] [[Category:Castles in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Mōri clan]] [[Category:Ruined castles in Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

{{coord|34|41|34.8|N|133|49|19.36|E|region:JP_scale:10000_source:jawiki|display=inline}} | height= | ownership= | controlledby={{ill|Ishikawa clan|en|Ishikawa clan|WD=Q615039|lt=Ishikawa clan}} (until 1575), {{ill|Mōri clan|en|Mōri clan|WD=Q914637|lt=Mōri clan}} (1575–1582), {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} (1582–1598), [[Hanabusa clan]] (c. 1598 – mid-17th century) | condition=ruins | built=late 16th century | builder=[[Mimura clan]] | used=late 16th to mid-17th centuries | materials=Wood, stone, plaster | demolished= | battles={{ill|Siege of Takamatsu|en|Siege of Takamatsu|WD=Q1046201|lt=Siege of Takamatsu}} (1582) | events= | past_commanders={{ill|Shimizu Muneharu|en|Shimizu Muneharu|WD=Q1046032|lt=Shimizu Muneharu}} (c. 1575–1582)
[[Hanabusa Masanari]] | garrison= | occupants= |footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} in his 1582 {{ill|Siege of Takamatsu|en|Siege of Takamatsu|WD=Q1046201|lt=siege}} of the castle]] {{nihongo|'''Takamatsu Castle'''|高松城|Takamatsu-jō}} of {{ill|Bitchū Province|en|Bitchū Province|WD=Q1047514|lt=Bitchū Province}} was a {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}} {{ill|Japanese castle|en|Japanese castle|WD=Q92026|lt=Japanese castle}} located in what is today the Kayo neighborhood of {{ill|Kita-ku, Okayama|en|Kita-ku, Okayama|WD=Q855478|lt=Kita-ku, Okayama}} in {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture}}.{{cite web|URL=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%82%99%E4%B8%AD%E9%AB%98%E6%9D%BE%E5%9F%8E-180261|title= 備中高松城（読み）びっちゅうたかまつじょう百科事典マイペディア「備中高松城」の解説|publisher=kotobank|access-date=15 October 2021}} The style of the castle was a ''hirajō'' (flatland castle) with no stone walls, but only earthen walls. The castle was surrounded by marshes, which formed a natural moat. Its ruins have been protected by the central government as a [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 1902.{{cite web|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2260|title=高松城跡 附 水攻築提跡 |work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 December 2016}} ==History== It is not clear when a castle was built at this location, which is located between central Okayama city and the Takahashi River. Historically this area had been a granary area and the main base of ancient [[Kingdom of Kibi]]. The route of the {{ill|San'yōdō|en|San'yōdō|WD=Q1376722|lt=San'yōdō}} highway which connected {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} with {{ill|Shimonoseki|en|Shimonoseki|WD=Q203308|lt=Shimonoseki}} passed through natural levee just at south of the castle. During the early Sengoku period, the Mimura clan ruled the entire Bitchu region from Bitchu-Matsuyama Castle, and the Mimura ordered their vassals, the Ishikawa clan, to build a fortification at this site. The Ishikawa and Mimura were destroyed by the {{ill|Mōri clan|en|Mōri clan|WD=Q914637|lt=Mōri clan}} in 1575, and the castle was awarded to {{ill|Shimizu Muneharu|en|Shimizu Muneharu|WD=Q1046032|lt=Shimizu Muneharu}}, a senior vassal of the Ishikawa who had defected to the Mōri. In 1582, {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Hashiba Hideyoshi}} was ordered to conquer western Japan by {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}} and laid {{ill|Siege of Takamatsu|en|Siege of Takamatsu|WD=Q1046201|lt=siege to Takamatsu Castle}}. As Takamatsu Castle was surrounded by marshes, it was considered impregnable to a normal assault and was expected to be able to withstand a prolonged siege. However, Hideyoshi adopted an innovative approach at the suggestion of his strategist [[:en:Kuroda Kanbei|Kuroda Kanbei]]. By paying extravagant amounts of money for round-the-clock efforts, he completed a three-kilometer long dike, damming the outlets to the marshes within 11 days. Aided by the arrival of the {{ill|East Asian rainy season|en|East Asian rainy season|WD=Q658486|lt=rainy season}}, the castle was soon flooded. Shimizu Muneharu was forced to surrender, committing {{ill|Seppuku|en|Seppuku|WD=Q133462|lt=Seppuku}} in exchange for the lives of his troops. Following this siege, and the rise of Toyotomi Hideyoshi to power after the assassination of Oda Nobunaga, the castle became part of the territory of {{ill|Ukita Hideie|en|Ukita Hideie|WD=Q1329792|lt=Ukita Hideie}}, who assigned it to his ''{{ill|Karō|en|Karō|WD=Q1316445|lt=Karō}}'', [[Hanabusa Masashige]]. Following the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara}} in 1600, the Ukita clan sided with the losing Western army; however, the [[Hanabusa clan]] fought alongside the Eastern Army of {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, and were awarded ''{{ill|Hatamoto|en|Hatamoto|WD=Q572662|lt=Hatamoto}}'' status as direct retainers of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. Some years later the Hanabusa relocated from Takamatsu to Abe, in what is today {{ill|Sōja|en|Sōja|WD=Q873536|lt=Sōja}} city and the castle was abandoned. Today, only a portion of the dam built by Hideyoshi and a small part of the castle's masonry has survived, and almost no trace of the castle remains. The ruins include the alleged Kofuns for Shimizu Muneharu's head, and for his body. The site has been maintained as the {{nihongo|'''Takamatsu Castle Siege Historic Site Park'''|高松城水攻め史跡公園|Takamatsu-jō mizuzeme shiseki kōen}}, with markers, such as wooden posts, show the area where the castle, dikes, and siege equipment were once located and where Shimizu Muneharu committed suicide. On April 6, 2017 Bitchu Takamatsu Castle was designated by the Japanese Castle Foundation as Okayama Prefecture's only entry on the [[:en:Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles|Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles]] (続日本100名城). The list adds to the original Top 100 list totaling 200 castles. Bitchu Takamatsu Castle is listed as #171.{{cite web|url= http://jokaku.jp/japan-top-100-castles/best-100-castles-of-japan-2nd-selection/ |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 July 2019}} The castle ruins are a ten minutes walk from {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Kibi Line|en|Kibi Line|WD=Q844682|lt=Kibi Line}} [[:en:Bitchu-Takamatsu Station|Bitchu-Takamatsu Station]]. == In popular culture == Takamatsu Castle was featured in the video game ''{{ill|Soulcalibur (video game)|en|Soulcalibur (video game)|WD=Q1060715|lt=Soulcalibur}}'' by [[Namco]], used as the fighting stage for the character {{ill|Heishiro Mitsurugi|en|Heishiro Mitsurugi|WD=Q60533066|lt=Heishiro Mitsurugi}}. The game contains two versions of the stage, one set during the siege and the other set in the winter following it. ==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Okayama)]] == Literature == *{{cite book |title=An Encyclopedia of Japanese Castles | last=De Lange |first=William |year=2021 |pages=600 pages|publisher=Toyo Press |location=Groningen |isbn=978-9492722300}} * Sansom, George (1961). ''A History of Japan: 1334–1615''. Stanford, California: Stanford University Press * Turnbull, Stephen (1998). ''The Samurai Sourcebook''. London: Cassell & Co. == References == {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category-inline|Takamatsu Castle (Bitchu)}} *[https://www.pref.okayama.jp/site/kodai/628728.html Okayama Ancient Kibi Cultural Properties Center] {{Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles}} [[file:Kawazugahana Embankment base.jpg|thumb|An excavated portion of the dike and embankment base constructed by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} [[en:Takamatsu Castle (Bitchū)]] {{Commons category|Takamatsu Castle (Bitchu)}} [[es:Castillo Takamatsu (Bitchu)]] [[fa:قلعه تاکاماتسو]] [[fr:Château de Takamatsu (Bitchu)]] [[ja:高松城 (備中国)]] [[ko:다카마쓰성 (오카야마현)]] [[pt:Castelo de Takamatsu (Bitchu)]] [[zh:高松城 (備中國)]] {{wikidata link|Q1071073}} [[Category:Bitchū Province]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Okayama]] [[Category:Castles in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Mōri clan]] [[Category:Ruined castles in Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

Historically this area had been a granary area and the main base of ancient [[Kingdom of Kibi]]. The route of the {{ill|San'yōdō|en|San'yōdō|WD=Q1376722|lt=San'yōdō|1=San'yōdō}} highway which connected {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} with {{ill|Shimonoseki|en|Shimonoseki|WD=Q203308|lt=Shimonoseki|1=Shimonoseki}} passed through natural levee just at south of the castle. By paying extravagant amounts of money for round-the-clock efforts, he completed a three-kilometer long dike, damming the outlets to the marshes within 11 days. Aided by the arrival of the {{ill|East Asian rainy season|en|East Asian rainy season|WD=Q658486|lt=rainy season|1=East Asian rainy season}}, the castle was soon flooded. Shimizu Muneharu was forced to surrender, committing {{ill|Seppuku|en|Seppuku|WD=Q133462|lt=Seppuku|1=Seppuku}} in exchange for the lives of his troops. Following this siege, and the rise of Toyotomi Hideyoshi to power after the assassination of Oda Nobunaga, the castle became part of the territory of {{ill|Ukita Hideie|en|Ukita Hideie|WD=Q1329792|lt=Ukita Hideie|1=Ukita Hideie}}, who assigned it to his ''{{ill|Karō|en|Karō|WD=Q1316445|lt=Karō|1=Karō}}'', [[Hanabusa Masashige]]. Following the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara|1=Battle of Sekigahara}} in 1600, the Ukita clan sided with the losing Western army; however, the [[Hanabusa clan]] fought alongside the Eastern Army of {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, and were awarded ''{{ill|Hatamoto|en|Hatamoto|WD=Q572662|lt=Hatamoto|1=Hatamoto}}'' status as direct retainers of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. The site has been maintained as the {{nihongo|'''Takamatsu Castle Siege Historic Site Park'''|高松城水攻め史跡公園|Takamatsu-jō mizuzeme shiseki kōen}}, with markers, such as wooden posts, show the area where the castle, dikes, and siege equipment were once located and where Shimizu Muneharu committed suicide. The list adds to the original Top 100 list totaling 200 castles. Bitchu Takamatsu Castle is listed as #171.{{cite web|url= http://jokaku.jp/japan-top-100-castles/best-100-castles-of-japan-2nd-selection/ |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 July 2019}} The castle ruins are a ten minutes walk from {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company|1=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Kibi Line|en|Kibi Line|WD=Q844682|lt=Kibi Line|1=Kibi Line}} [[:en:Bitchu-Takamatsu Station|Bitchu-Takamatsu Station]]. == In popular culture == Takamatsu Castle was featured in the video game ''{{ill|Soulcalibur (video game)|en|Soulcalibur (video game)|WD=Q1060715|lt=Soulcalibur|1=Soulcalibur (video game)}}'' by [[Namco]], used as the fighting stage for the character {{ill|Heishiro Mitsurugi|en|Heishiro Mitsurugi|WD=Q60533066|lt=Heishiro Mitsurugi|1=Heishiro Mitsurugi}}. [[Hanabusa Masanari]] | garrison= | occupants= |footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} in his 1582 {{ill|Siege of Takamatsu|en|Siege of Takamatsu|WD=Q1046201|lt=siege|1=Siege of Takamatsu}} of the castle]] {{nihongo|'''Takamatsu Castle'''|高松城|Takamatsu-jō}} of {{ill|Bitchū Province|en|Bitchū Province|WD=Q1047514|lt=Bitchū Province|1=Bitchū Province}} was a {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}} {{ill|Japanese castle|en|Japanese castle|WD=Q92026|lt=Japanese castle|1=Japanese castle}} located in what is today the Kayo neighborhood of {{ill|Kita-ku, Okayama|en|Kita-ku, Okayama|WD=Q855478|lt=Kita-ku, Okayama|1=Kita-ku, Okayama}} in {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture|1=Okayama Prefecture}}.{{cite web|URL=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%82%99%E4%B8%AD%E9%AB%98%E6%9D%BE%E5%9F%8E-180261|title= 備中高松城（読み）びっちゅうたかまつじょう百科事典マイペディア「備中高松城」の解説|publisher=kotobank|access-date=15 October 2021}} The style of the castle was a ''hirajō'' (flatland castle) with no stone walls, but only earthen walls. The castle was surrounded by marshes, which formed a natural moat. Its ruins have been protected by the central government as a [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 1902.{{cite web|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2260|title=高松城跡 附 水攻築提跡 |work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 December 2016}} ==History== It is not clear when a castle was built at this location, which is located between central Okayama city and the Takahashi River. Historically this area had been a granary area and the main base of ancient [[Kingdom of Kibi]]. The route of the {{ill|San'yōdō|en|San'yōdō|WD=Q1376722|lt=San'yōdō|1=San'yōdō}} highway which connected {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} with {{ill|Shimonoseki|en|Shimonoseki|WD=Q203308|lt=Shimonoseki|1=Shimonoseki}} passed through natural levee just at south of the castle. During the early Sengoku period, the Mimura clan ruled the entire Bitchu region from Bitchu-Matsuyama Castle, and the Mimura ordered their vassals, the Ishikawa clan, to build a fortification at this site. The Ishikawa and Mimura were destroyed by the {{ill|Mōri clan|en|Mōri clan|WD=Q914637|lt=Mōri clan|1=Mōri clan}} in 1575, and the castle was awarded to {{ill|Shimizu Muneharu|en|Shimizu Muneharu|WD=Q1046032|lt=Shimizu Muneharu|1=Shimizu Muneharu}}, a senior vassal of the Ishikawa who had defected to the Mōri. In 1582, {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Hashiba Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} was ordered to conquer western Japan by {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}} and laid {{ill|Siege of Takamatsu|en|Siege of Takamatsu|WD=Q1046201|lt=siege to Takamatsu Castle|1=Siege of Takamatsu}}. As Takamatsu Castle was surrounded by marshes, it was considered impregnable to a normal assault and was expected to be able to withstand a prolonged siege. However, Hideyoshi adopted an innovative approach at the suggestion of his strategist [[:en:Kuroda Kanbei|Kuroda Kanbei]]. By paying extravagant amounts of money for round-the-clock efforts, he completed a three-kilometer long dike, damming the outlets to the marshes within 11 days. Aided by the arrival of the {{ill|East Asian rainy season|en|East Asian rainy season|WD=Q658486|lt=rainy season|1=East Asian rainy season}}, the castle was soon flooded. Shimizu Muneharu was forced to surrender, committing {{ill|Seppuku|en|Seppuku|WD=Q133462|lt=Seppuku|1=Seppuku}} in exchange for the lives of his troops. Following this siege, and the rise of Toyotomi Hideyoshi to power after the assassination of Oda Nobunaga, the castle became part of the territory of {{ill|Ukita Hideie|en|Ukita Hideie|WD=Q1329792|lt=Ukita Hideie|1=Ukita Hideie}}, who assigned it to his ''{{ill|Karō|en|Karō|WD=Q1316445|lt=Karō|1=Karō}}'', [[Hanabusa Masashige]]. Following the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara|1=Battle of Sekigahara}} in 1600, the Ukita clan sided with the losing Western army; however, the [[Hanabusa clan]] fought alongside the Eastern Army of {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, and were awarded ''{{ill|Hatamoto|en|Hatamoto|WD=Q572662|lt=Hatamoto|1=Hatamoto}}'' status as direct retainers of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. Some years later the Hanabusa relocated from Takamatsu to Abe, in what is today {{ill|Sōja|en|Sōja|WD=Q873536|lt=Sōja|1=Sōja}} city and the castle was abandoned. Today, only a portion of the dam built by Hideyoshi and a small part of the castle's masonry has survived, and almost no trace of the castle remains. The ruins include the alleged Kofuns for Shimizu Muneharu's head, and for his body. The site has been maintained as the {{nihongo|'''Takamatsu Castle Siege Historic Site Park'''|高松城水攻め史跡公園|Takamatsu-jō mizuzeme shiseki kōen}}, with markers, such as wooden posts, show the area where the castle, dikes, and siege equipment were once located and where Shimizu Muneharu committed suicide. On April 6, 2017 Bitchu Takamatsu Castle was designated by the Japanese Castle Foundation as Okayama Prefecture's only entry on the [[:en:Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles|Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles]] (続日本100名城). The list adds to the original Top 100 list totaling 200 castles. Bitchu Takamatsu Castle is listed as #171.{{cite web|url= http://jokaku.jp/japan-top-100-castles/best-100-castles-of-japan-2nd-selection/ |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 July 2019}} The castle ruins are a ten minutes walk from {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company|1=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Kibi Line|en|Kibi Line|WD=Q844682|lt=Kibi Line|1=Kibi Line}} [[:en:Bitchu-Takamatsu Station|Bitchu-Takamatsu Station]]. == In popular culture == Takamatsu Castle was featured in the video game ''{{ill|Soulcalibur (video game)|en|Soulcalibur (video game)|WD=Q1060715|lt=Soulcalibur|1=Soulcalibur (video game)}}'' by [[Namco]], used as the fighting stage for the character {{ill|Heishiro Mitsurugi|en|Heishiro Mitsurugi|WD=Q60533066|lt=Heishiro Mitsurugi|1=Heishiro Mitsurugi}}. The game contains two versions of the stage, one set during the siege and the other set in the winter following it. ==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Okayama)]] == Literature == *{{cite book |title=An Encyclopedia of Japanese Castles | last=De Lange |first=William |year=2021 |pages=600 pages|publisher=Toyo Press |location=Groningen |isbn=978-9492722300}} * Sansom, George (1961). ''A History of Japan: 1334–1615''. Stanford, California: Stanford University Press * Turnbull, Stephen (1998). ''The Samurai Sourcebook''. London: Cassell & Co. == References == {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category-inline|Takamatsu Castle (Bitchu)}} *[https://www.pref.okayama.jp/site/kodai/628728.html Okayama Ancient Kibi Cultural Properties Center] {{Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles}} [[file:Kawazugahana Embankment base.jpg|thumb|An excavated portion of the dike and embankment base constructed by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} [[en:Takamatsu Castle (Bitchū)]] {{Commons category|Takamatsu Castle (Bitchu)}} [[es:Castillo Takamatsu (Bitchu)]] [[fa:قلعه تاکاماتسو]] [[fr:Château de Takamatsu (Bitchu)]] [[ja:高松城 (備中国)]] [[ko:다카마쓰성 (오카야마현)]] [[pt:Castelo de Takamatsu (Bitchu)]] [[zh:高松城 (備中國)]] {{wikidata link|Q1071073}} [[Category:Bitchū Province]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Okayama]] [[Category:Castles in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Mōri clan]] [[Category:Ruined castles in Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

[[Hanabusa Masanari]] | garrison= | occupants= |footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} in his 1582 {{ill|Siege of Takamatsu|en|Siege of Takamatsu|WD=Q1046201|lt=siege|1=Siege of Takamatsu}} of the castle]] {{nihongo|'''Takamatsu Castle'''|高松城|Takamatsu-jō}} of {{ill|Bitchū Province|en|Bitchū Province|WD=Q1047514|lt=Bitchū Province|1=Bitchū Province}} was a {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}} {{ill|Japanese castle|en|Japanese castle|WD=Q92026|lt=Japanese castle|1=Japanese castle}} located in what is today the Kayo neighborhood of {{ill|Kita-ku, Okayama|en|Kita-ku, Okayama|WD=Q855478|lt=Kita-ku, Okayama|1=Kita-ku, Okayama}} in {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture|1=Okayama Prefecture}}.{{cite web|URL=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%82%99%E4%B8%AD%E9%AB%98%E6%9D%BE%E5%9F%8E-180261|title= 備中高松城（読み）びっちゅうたかまつじょう百科事典マイペディア「備中高松城」の解説|publisher=kotobank|access-date=15 October 2021}} The style of the castle was a ''hirajō'' (flatland castle) with no stone walls, but only earthen walls. The castle was surrounded by marshes, which formed a natural moat. Its ruins have been protected by the central government as a [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 1902.{{cite web|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2260|title=高松城跡 附 水攻築提跡 |work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 December 2016}} ==History== It is not clear when a castle was built at this location, which is located between central Okayama city and the Takahashi River. Historically this area had been a granary area and the main base of ancient [[Kingdom of Kibi]]. The route of the {{ill|San'yōdō|en|San'yōdō|WD=Q1376722|lt=San'yōdō|1=San'yōdō}} highway which connected {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} with {{ill|Shimonoseki|en|Shimonoseki|WD=Q203308|lt=Shimonoseki|1=Shimonoseki}} passed through natural levee just at south of the castle. During the early Sengoku period, the Mimura clan ruled the entire Bitchu region from Bitchu-Matsuyama Castle, and the Mimura ordered their vassals, the Ishikawa clan, to build a fortification at this site. The Ishikawa and Mimura were destroyed by the {{ill|Mōri clan|en|Mōri clan|WD=Q914637|lt=Mōri clan|1=Mōri clan}} in 1575, and the castle was awarded to {{ill|Shimizu Muneharu|en|Shimizu Muneharu|WD=Q1046032|lt=Shimizu Muneharu|1=Shimizu Muneharu}}, a senior vassal of the Ishikawa who had defected to the Mōri. In 1582, {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Hashiba Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} was ordered to conquer western Japan by {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}} and laid {{ill|Siege of Takamatsu|en|Siege of Takamatsu|WD=Q1046201|lt=siege to Takamatsu Castle|1=Siege of Takamatsu}}. As Takamatsu Castle was surrounded by marshes, it was considered impregnable to a normal assault and was expected to be able to withstand a prolonged siege. However, Hideyoshi adopted an innovative approach at the suggestion of his strategist [[:en:Kuroda Kanbei|Kuroda Kanbei]]. By paying extravagant amounts of money for round-the-clock efforts, he completed a three-kilometer long dike, damming the outlets to the marshes within 11 days. Aided by the arrival of the {{ill|East Asian rainy season|en|East Asian rainy season|WD=Q658486|lt=rainy season|1=East Asian rainy season}}, the castle was soon flooded. Shimizu Muneharu was forced to surrender, committing {{ill|Seppuku|en|Seppuku|WD=Q133462|lt=Seppuku|1=Seppuku}} in exchange for the lives of his troops. Following this siege, and the rise of Toyotomi Hideyoshi to power after the assassination of Oda Nobunaga, the castle became part of the territory of {{ill|Ukita Hideie|en|Ukita Hideie|WD=Q1329792|lt=Ukita Hideie|1=Ukita Hideie}}, who assigned it to his ''{{ill|Karō|en|Karō|WD=Q1316445|lt=Karō|1=Karō}}'', [[Hanabusa Masashige]]. Following the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara|1=Battle of Sekigahara}} in 1600, the Ukita clan sided with the losing Western army; however, the [[Hanabusa clan]] fought alongside the Eastern Army of {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, and were awarded ''{{ill|Hatamoto|en|Hatamoto|WD=Q572662|lt=Hatamoto|1=Hatamoto}}'' status as direct retainers of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. Some years later the Hanabusa relocated from Takamatsu to Abe, in what is today {{ill|Sōja|en|Sōja|WD=Q873536|lt=Sōja|1=Sōja}} city and the castle was abandoned. Today, only a portion of the dam built by Hideyoshi and a small part of the castle's masonry has survived, and almost no trace of the castle remains. The ruins include the alleged Kofuns for Shimizu Muneharu's head, and for his body. The site has been maintained as the {{nihongo|'''Takamatsu Castle Siege Historic Site Park'''|高松城水攻め史跡公園|Takamatsu-jō mizuzeme shiseki kōen}}, with markers, such as wooden posts, show the area where the castle, dikes, and siege equipment were once located and where Shimizu Muneharu committed suicide. On April 6, 2017 Bitchu Takamatsu Castle was designated by the Japanese Castle Foundation as Okayama Prefecture's only entry on the [[:en:Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles|Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles]] (続日本100名城). The list adds to the original Top 100 list totaling 200 castles. Bitchu Takamatsu Castle is listed as #171.{{cite web|url= http://jokaku.jp/japan-top-100-castles/best-100-castles-of-japan-2nd-selection/ |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 July 2019}} The castle ruins are a ten minutes walk from {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company|1=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Kibi Line|en|Kibi Line|WD=Q844682|lt=Kibi Line|1=Kibi Line}} [[:en:Bitchu-Takamatsu Station|Bitchu-Takamatsu Station]]. == In popular culture == Takamatsu Castle was featured in the video game ''{{ill|Soulcalibur (video game)|en|Soulcalibur (video game)|WD=Q1060715|lt=Soulcalibur|1=Soulcalibur (video game)}}'' by [[Namco]], used as the fighting stage for the character {{ill|Heishiro Mitsurugi|en|Heishiro Mitsurugi|WD=Q60533066|lt=Heishiro Mitsurugi|1=Heishiro Mitsurugi}}. The game contains two versions of the stage, one set during the siege and the other set in the winter following it. ==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Okayama)]] == Literature == *{{cite book |title=An Encyclopedia of Japanese Castles | last=De Lange |first=William |year=2021 |pages=600 pages|publisher=Toyo Press |location=Groningen |isbn=978-9492722300}} * Sansom, George (1961). ''A History of Japan: 1334–1615''. Stanford, California: Stanford University Press * Turnbull, Stephen (1998). ''The Samurai Sourcebook''. London: Cassell & Co. == References == {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category-inline|Takamatsu Castle (Bitchu)}} *[https://www.pref.okayama.jp/site/kodai/628728.html Okayama Ancient Kibi Cultural Properties Center] {{Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles}} [[file:Kawazugahana Embankment base.jpg|thumb|An excavated portion of the dike and embankment base constructed by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} [[en:Takamatsu Castle (Bitchū)]] {{Commons category|Takamatsu Castle (Bitchu)}} [[es:Castillo Takamatsu (Bitchu)]] [[fa:قلعه تاکاماتسو]] [[fr:Château de Takamatsu (Bitchu)]] [[ja:高松城 (備中国)]] [[ko:다카마쓰성 (오카야마현)]] [[pt:Castelo de Takamatsu (Bitchu)]] [[zh:高松城 (備中國)]] {{wikidata link|Q1071073}} [[Category:Bitchū Province]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Okayama]] [[Category:Castles in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Mōri clan]] [[Category:Ruined castles in Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
